sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Wayne, presented with a sore on his right foot that has not been healing for approximately two to three weeks.
He reports that the wound initially started as a blister from a new pair of shoes, but it has since worsened.
He denies any trauma to the foot and has been applying a band-aid to the area.
He reports that the wound does not hurt and is not accompanied by any symptoms such as fever, chills, or drainage.
He also mentions that the wound sticks to his sock when he removes it.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes, with an HbA1c of a little over 8.
He has not seen a podiatrist in the past two years due to the previous one moving.
Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms, but mentions that he has had issues with stability in the past due to his shoes.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's temperature is 98.1, heart rate is 72, respirations are 16, and blood pressure is 110/65.

Physical exam: The examination reveals a 1x2 inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the right fifth MTP joint, with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue.
There is no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, and no evidence of fluid collection, necrosis, or odor.
Bony exposure is not appreciated.
Vascular exam shows palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses, but no palpable DP or PT pulses, with Doppler signs present.
Test Results: The patient's right foot X-ray shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer on the right foot, which is not healing due to potential issues with blood supply.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; suspected inadequate blood supply; unknown diabetes status.
Plan:

I prescribed collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily, covered with a dry sterile dressing, and will continue this treatment until the wound shrinks and a nice pink tissue is present.
I also performed a debridement in the office to remove dead tissue.
I ordered an ankle-brachial index (ABI) to determine the blood supply to the foot.
I will refer the patient to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates inadequate blood supply for wound healing.
I will see the patient again in two weeks.
I also plan to refer the patient to a diabetic specialist to discuss ways to modify his diet and improve his HbA1c levels.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in two weeks to reassess the wound and determine the need for further treatment.
